preamble: The transformation from a voiced aspirated bilabial plosive /bʱ/ to a voiceless labiodental fricative /f/ represents a significant lenition process involving multiple phonetic changes. While a direct, single-step historical shift of /bʱ/ to /f/ is not widely documented as a regular sound law, this change is phonetically plausible and can be understood as a sequence of two well-attested historical shifts: the lenition of /bʱ/ to a voiced labiodental fricative /v/, followed by the common phonetic process of devoicing of /v/ to /f/.
phoneticEffects: This transformation involves several key phonetic changes: deaspiration (loss of aspiration), lenition (the stop articulation of /bʱ/ is weakened to a fricative articulation), devoicing (the voiced quality of the sound is lost, resulting in a voiceless fricative), and a slight place shift from bilabial to labiodental

examples.note: In the Kharia language, the voiced labiodental fricative /v/ historically developed from an earlier voiced aspirated bilabial plosive */bʱ/.
In Bengali, the voiced aspirated bilabial plosive /bʱ/ can be phonetically realized as a voiced bilabial or labiodental fricative [β~v], particularly in some varieties.
